elements.sent-2411-001: We present a hierarchical framework to solve robot planning as an input control problem.
elements.sent-2411-002: At the lowest level are temporary closed control loops, (``tasks''), each representing a behaviour, contingent on a specific sensory input and therefore temporary.
elements.sent-2411-003: At the highest level, a supervising ``Configurator'' directs task creation and termination.
elements.sent-2411-004: Here resides ``core'' knowledge as a physics engine, where sequences of tasks can be simulated.
elements.sent-2411-005: The Configurator encodes and interprets simulation results, based on which it can choose a sequence of tasks as a plan.
elements.sent-2411-006: We implement this framework on a real robot and test it in an overtaking scenario as proof-of-concept.
elements.sent-2411-007: Living organisms interact with their surroundings through sensory inputs in a closed-loop fashion \citep{Maturana1980 .}
elements.sent-2411-008: To achieve basic closed-loop navigation in a robot it is sufficient to directly connect a robot's sensors to its motor effectors, and the specific excitatory or inhibitory connections determine the control strategy~\citep{Braitenberg1986 .}
elements.sent-2411-009: These sensor-effector connections represent a single closed-loop controller, which produces stereotyped behaviour in response to an immediate stimulus.
elements.sent-2411-010: The state-of-the-art for autonomous navigation falls into the categories of closed-loop output control (e.g. Reinforcement Learning, RL), or trajectory planning (see~\cite{Karur2021 ).}
elements.sent-2411-011: In closed-loop output control, an agent uses environmental feedback to learn to associate input states with actions, aiming to maximise the cumulative reward.
elements.sent-2411-012: Feedback is provided at the end of each training episode, which makes a RL model inherently slow to train.
elements.sent-2411-013: Trajectory planners represent, on the other hand, open-loop controllers.
elements.sent-2411-014: Environment feedback may be provided on trajectory following.
elements.sent-2411-015: As an embodied agent is oblivious to its trajectory, this requires an external observed (e.g. a camera on the ceiling).
elements.sent-2411-016: Closed-loop behaviours (CLB) have some advantages over the state-of-the-art.
elements.sent-2411-017: First, the behaviour displayed by a robot depends on hard-wired connections; thus, responses to inputs do not need to be learned from scratch through lengthy training.
elements.sent-2411-018: Secondly, to implement CLB, a robot only needs information about the location of an object relative to itself to successfully perform a control manoeuvre.
elements.sent-2411-019: On the other hand, as a single control loop only receives information about an object at a time, it is not, by itself, suitable for the formulation of complex navigation plans.
elements.sent-2411-020: In this work, we propose a framework where CLB such as described in~\citep{Braitenberg1986 are combined with core knowledge to achieve multi-step ahead planning in an embodied, situated agent.}
elements.sent-2411-021: Fig.~\ref{fig:task provides a visual representation of a task.}
elements.sent-2411-022: As shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:task B, a task is a closed-loop behaviour contingent on a disturbance $D$ which enters the environment $P$ and generates an error signal $E$ via the robot's sensors.}
elements.sent-2411-023: As long as signal $E$ is nonzero, the agent $H$ produces a continuous motor output $M$ aimed at counteracting the disturbance (see Fig.~\ref{fig:task A).}
elements.sent-2411-024: Otherwise, the control behaviour is no longer necessary and it terminates.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.5\textwidth]{figures/NECO-001.eps} \caption{A: closed-loop obstacle avoidance. B: a closed-loop controller.} \label{fig:task}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figures/NECO-002.eps} \caption{ Example of the multi-step-ahead planning procedure carried out by the Configurator. A: a scenario requiring multi-step planning, B: Configurator, C: the plan, D: expansion of the start state, E: expansion of bext next state, F: full state-space expansion, G: state diagram of panel E, H: full state diagram.} \label{fig:react_loop}
elements.sent-2411-025: Fig.~\ref{fig:react_loop illustrates the role of the Configurator in the planning process.}
elements.sent-2411-026: Fig.~\ref{fig:react_loop A presents a scenario requiring multi-step planning: a robot is driving straight (task $T_S$) aimed at reaching the target $D_G$ in an environment where obstacles are present in front of it and to its right.}
elements.sent-2411-027: The Configurator (Fig.~\ref{fig:react_loop B) thus initiates planning.}
elements.sent-2411-028: First, the environment has to be explored in order to know what tasks are feasible to perform to reach the goal.
elements.sent-2411-029: As denoted by the thought balloon, this exploration is not actual but simulated using core knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-030: Fig.~\ref{fig:react_loop D presents the first stage of the exploration process.}
elements.sent-2411-031: From the present task, the robot has the option to proceed straight (task $T_S$), turn left (task $T_L$) and then drive straight, or turn right (task $T_R$) and then go straight.
elements.sent-2411-032: The first and second options result in collisions (marked as stars) with the obstacle in front, while the third can be executed safely.
elements.sent-2411-033: These task sequences are represented in Fig.~\ref{fig:react_loop E: the control strategies represented by each task ($T_S, T_L, T_S$) are inscribed in the corresponding control loop.}
elements.sent-2411-034: To motivate the existence of a CLB, the Configurator injects the goal $D_G$ as a disturbance in each task.
elements.sent-2411-035: Tasks whose execution is interrupted by a collision are marked with stars.
elements.sent-2411-036: Tasks and disturbances associated with them are collected in structures $q$ containing tasks and their disturbances, where the subscript indices indicate the order of task creation.
elements.sent-2411-037: As shown in figure~\ref{fig:react_loop F, the next stage of exploration starts from the task and disturbances in structure $q_3$ in a modular fashion following the steps in Fig.~\ref{fig:react_loop E.}}
elements.sent-2411-038: Fig.~\ref{fig:react_loop G depicts the full extent of the state-space expansion.}
elements.sent-2411-039: Note that after one bout of modular (simulated) exploration, even if no collisions are encountered (see states $q_6, q_8, q_10$, the exploration starts from the state which brings the robot closest to the goal (in this case $q_8$).
elements.sent-2411-040: Fig.~\ref{fig:react_loop H depicts the collection of the explored task sequences in form of a cognitive map.}
elements.sent-2411-041: This structure is searchable and a plan (bold arrows) can be extracted.
elements.sent-2411-042: Each task is queued for execution on the real robot, as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:react_loop C.}
elements.sent-2411-043: A state is a tuple $q=(T, D)$, where $T$ is a task and $D$ is a finite, possibly empty, set of disturbances.
elements.sent-2411-044: In set $D$ we use $D_I$ to indicate the disturbance to which the task is contingent, and $D_N$ to define a disturbance which interrupts the task execution.
elements.sent-2411-045: Set $\mathbf{T}$ is the task space such that $T \in \mathbf{T}$.
elements.sent-2411-046: Set $\mathbf{D}$ is the set of all disturbances such that $D \in \mathbf{D}$.
elements.sent-2411-047: A cognitive map is a tuple $(Q, \hookrightarrow)$, where $Q:\mathbf{T \times 2^{\mathbf{D }$ is a set of states and $\hookrightarrow:Q \times Q$ is a set of transition relations.}}
elements.sent-2411-048: The physics engine Box2D\footnote{\url{https://box2d.org/ represents the core knowledge using which sequences of tasks can be simulated.}}
elements.sent-2411-049: Objects in the Box2D simulation are constructed from ego-centric coordinates captured by the robot's LiDAR sensor.
elements.sent-2411-050: The raw point cloud is resampled so that only the points in the way of the present task are represented.
elements.sent-2411-051: A model of the robot is also present in the environment.
elements.sent-2411-052: At the beginning of the simulation, the robot is located at the origin of the plane.
elements.sent-2411-053: In this work, we define reset \begin{equation}R(q_1, q_2):D'_I= D_G \ \text{if} \ D_N= \emptyset\end{equation} where $D'_I$ is the disturbance to be injected in the task in state $q_2$, and $D_N$ is new disturbance which interrupted the task in state $q_1$.
elements.sent-2411-054: In other words, unless the task in $q_1$ is interrupted, the next task in $q_2$ will be contingent on the goal $D_G$.
elements.sent-2411-055: As we seek to plan to avoid collisions, transitions from tasks where set $D_N\neq \emptyset$ are not permitted.
elements.sent-2411-056: The reset for this case is not defined.
elements.sent-2411-057: As in~\citet{Hart1968 , we define a cost function $\phi: Q \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ such that \begin{equation}\phi(q) = \gamma(q)+\chi(q) \label{ev_function}\end{equation} where $q$ is a state in cognitive map $\mathcal{G}$, $\gamma(q)$ is a past cost function and $\chi(q)$ is a heuristic future cost function.}
elements.sent-2411-058: We calculate $\gamma(q)$ as the normalised distance from the start of the task to interrupting disturbance $D_N$, if present, and $\chi (q)$ as the normalised negative distance from the end of the task to goal $D_G$.
elements.sent-2411-059: Algorithm~\ref{e provides pseudocode for the construction of cognitive map $\mathcal{G}$.}
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Construction of the cognitive map $\mathcal{G}$} \begin{algorithmic} \State inputs: $\mathbf{D}_G$, \State initialise $Q={q_0}$, priority queue $PQ=\{q_{0}\}$, state to expand $q_{E}=q_{0}$, \Repeat \State remove \(q_E\) from \(PQ\) \State simulate all frontier states $q_F$ out of $q_E$ depth-first until $q_F=(T_S, \cdot)$ or $D_N \neq \emptyset$ \State add $q_{F}$ to $PQ$ \State set \(q_{E} = \min_{q \in PQ}\phi(q)\) \Until{no obstacle-free transitions exist out of $q_{E}$ or $D_{G}$ has been reached} \Return \(\mathcal{G}\) \end{algorithmic}\label{e}
elements.sent-2411-060: Guard $\Psi$ is used to extract a plan $\rho$ from cognitive map $\mathcal{G}$.
elements.sent-2411-061: Formally, the plan is \begin{equation}\rho = \{q_0, q_1...q_n \ | \ (q_i, q_{i+1}) \in \hookrightarrow \forall i \in \mathbb{N} \ , \ q_n= \min_{q \in Q} \phi(q)\}\end{equation} or a set of adjacent states where the last state has the least cost $\phi$ in the set of states $Q$.
elements.formula-2411-003: \begin{equation}\rho = \{q_0, q_1...q_n \ | \ (q_i, q_{i+1}) \in \hookrightarrow \forall i \in N \ , \ q_n= \min_{q \in Q} \phi(q)\}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-062: This results in the assignment of $\psi(q)=1$ if $q\in \rho$ and zero otherwise.
elements.sent-2411-063: We test the decision-making process in an overtaking scenario, where a target location $D_G$ located 1 meter in front of the robot must be reached in presence of an obstacle.
elements.sent-2411-064: We contrast our new approach against a reactive control strategy which uses a single closed-loop controller.
elements.sent-2411-065: In the planning condition, the robot was able to formulate a plan to reach the target in all 10 runs (example run depicted in Fig.~\ref{fig:tar_all A).}
elements.sent-2411-066: The mean time for state-space exploration and planning was 0.064 $\pm$ 0.009 seconds for a state-space comprised of 32.9 $\pm$ 1.758 states (see Fig.~\ref{fig:tar_all C for an example simulation trace with state labels).}
elements.sent-2411-067: During execution, the robot brushed the obstacle twice in the planning condition, both times in one particular scenario due to execution error accumulation.
elements.sent-2411-068: The robot never reached the target in the reactive condition (see Fig.~\ref{fig:tar_all B); moreover, it collided 8 times with obstacles over the ten runs.}
elements.sent-2411-069: Differences in number of collisions approached, but did not reach, statistical significance (paired T=-1.747, p=0.111), possibly due to the small sample size.
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=.75\textwidth]{figures/NECO-006.eps} \caption{ Initial frame and tracking of the behaviour exhibited by the robot. Targets are depicted as hearts, collisions as stars. A: multiple-loop planning, B: one-loop-ahead disturbance detection, C: simulation trace and state labels for planning condition, executed plan highlighted in bold. } \label{fig:tar_all}
elements.sent-2411-070: We have developed a framework to achieve multi-step ahead, real-time planning over CLB thanks to core knowledge as a physics simulation.
elements.sent-2411-071: This work showcases an innovative use of a physical simulation as a tool for an agent to actively reason over its environment.
elements.sent-2411-072: This allows for constructing a cognitive map without need for physical exploration, where the environment is discretised in terms of CLB afforded by objects in the environment.. \begin{footnotesize \bibliographystyle{unsrtnat \end{footnotesize.}}}
